name: Gwydion
description: Gwydion is a prominent figure in Welsh mythology, primarily known from the Mabinogion, particularly the Fourth Branch. He is depicted as a skilled magician, trickster, warrior, and bard, often associated with wisdom, knowledge, and illusion. Gwydion is the son of Dôn and the brother of Arianrhod, Gilfaethwy, and other figures. His birth is shrouded in mystery, and he is often portrayed as a cunning and resourceful individual. He is renowned for his ability to shape-shift, create illusions, and use magic to achieve his goals. One of his most famous feats is creating Lleu Llaw Gyffes, a son for his sister Arianrhod after she cursed herself never to have a child of mortal man. He cleverly circumvents her curses through trickery and magic. Gwydion's character is complex, embodying both positive and negative traits. He is a protector and provider, but also capable of manipulation and deception. His actions often have far-reaching consequences, shaping the destinies of other characters within the mythology. He is a significant figure in Welsh folklore and continues to be a source of inspiration and fascination.
subtitle: Master Magician and Trickster God

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Lleu Llaw Gyffes
keyMyths.description: Gwydion uses trickery and magic to circumvent his sister Arianrhod's curses and create a son, Lleu Llaw Gyffes, for her. He disguises himself and Lleu to gain access to Arianrhod and trick her into naming and arming Lleu.

keyMyths.title: The Battle of the Trees
keyMyths.description: Gwydion animates the trees of the forest to fight against Arawn, king of Annwn, in a great battle. He uses his magical abilities to give the trees life and strength, turning them into formidable warriors.

keyMyths.title: The Story of Math fab Mathonwy
keyMyths.description: Gwydion and his brother Gilfaethwy conspire to rape Goewin, the foot-holder of Math fab Mathonwy, leading to their punishment and transformation into animals. This tale highlights Gwydion's darker side and the consequences of his actions.

sources.author: Lady Charlotte Guest
